name: Django Reinhardt
abstract: Jean Reinhardt (23 January 1910 – 16 May 1953), known by his Romani nickname Django (French: [dʒãŋɡo ʁɛjnaʁt] or [dʒɑ̃ɡo ʁenɑʁt]), was a French jazz guitarist and composer. He was one of the first major jazz talents to emerge in Europe and has been hailed as one of its most significant exponents.

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: the main thing I got from Django Reinhardt was the fact that you could get such an individual sound on an instrument... what I got from him earlier on in my life was the idea that you could get your own sound if you try.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Hyman
about_quotes.evidence: I’ve heard it not long ago and I was surprised because I hadn’t really thought about him that way, in Django Reinhardt.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Hyman interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 17, 2001 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Laurence Juber
about_quotes.evidence: Oscar Aleman, who you know, is not necessarily as well known as Django Reinhardt, but is a great guitar player
about_quotes.doc_title: Laurence Juber interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, July 21, 2020 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Waters
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Django Reinhardt was just what I mentioned, like [an] exception -

about_quotes.speaker: Nat Hentoff
about_quotes.evidence: His lead guitarist’s favorite guitarist was Django Reinhardt.
about_quotes.doc_title: Nat Hentoff interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Arthur Briggs
about_quotes.evidence: in order to form the orchestra, I engaged Django Reinhardt... and Stephane Grapelly on piano-violin. Well, it so happened that... Grapelly would play his violin and Django would play his guitar.
